edges.description: Absorbed thallium rapidly redistributes throughout the body and reaches multiple target organs.
Distributed thallium enters cells by exploiting potassium-like physicochemical properties.
Distributed thallium also accumulates in keratin-rich tissues such as hair and nails.
Intracellular thallium disrupts core pathways of ATP generation.
Intracellular thallium interferes with sodium-potassium ATPase activity and membrane ion homeostasis.
Intracellular thallium also binds sulfhydryl-containing molecules and perturbs protein function.
Energetically vulnerable peripheral nerves develop toxic injury with painful sensorimotor dysfunction.
Ion-pump failure contributes to dysautonomia and central nervous system dysfunction in more severe poisoning.
Sulfhydryl-dependent molecular dysfunction contributes to progressive peripheral nerve injury.
Peripheral nerve injury causes the characteristic painful ascending neuropathy.
Autonomic neurotoxicity contributes to characteristic lower-gastrointestinal dysmotility.
Severe neurotoxicity can extend to central neurologic dysfunction and encephalopathy.
Keratin-associated accumulation contributes to direct hair follicle injury.
Hair follicle toxicity and keratin-associated accumulation lead to delayed diffuse alopecia.

nodes.description: Diffuse alopecia is a characteristic delayed manifestation of thallium poisoning and often becomes a key diagnostic clue after the initial toxic phase.

evidence.snippet: In humans the most characteristic sign of thallium toxicity is alopecia which usually appears in cases when death is delayed for 15-20 days.

nodes.description: Severe poisoning can cause confusion, delirium, or other alterations in mental status alongside peripheral neurotoxicity.

evidence.snippet: Many other findings such as an autonomic neuropathy, cranial nerve abnormalities, altered mental status, motor weakness, cardiac, hepatic, and renal effects are described, but are less specific.

nodes.description: In more severe poisoning, thallium toxicity extends beyond painful peripheral neuropathy into autonomic dysfunction and central nervous system injury.

evidence.snippet: Many other findings such as an autonomic neuropathy, cranial nerve abnormalities, altered mental status, motor weakness, cardiac, hepatic, and renal effects are described, but are less specific.
Some patients with delayed admission had significant symptoms associated with central nervous system damage

nodes.description: Once inside cells, thallium impairs glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation, creating a broad failure of energy production that contributes to multi-organ toxicity.

evidence.snippet: thallium interferes with energy production at essential steps in glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

nodes.description: Constipation is a characteristic gastrointestinal feature of thallium poisoning and may be more typical than diarrhea.

evidence.snippet: Unlike exposure to most metal salts, gastrointestinal symptoms of thallium toxicity are relatively minor, and constipation is more characteristic than diarrhoea.

nodes.description: Management begins with immediate cessation of exposure and removal of the toxic source to prevent ongoing absorption.

evidence.snippet: An adequate clinical approach can facilitate early diagnosis.

nodes.description: Thallium salts are rapidly and nearly completely absorbed after exposure, with gastrointestinal ingestion being the most common route to clinically important poisoning.

evidence.snippet: Thallium salts are rapidly and nearly completely absorbed by virtually all routes, with gastrointestinal exposure being the most common route to produce toxicity.

nodes.description: Thallium causes dystrophic anagen injury in hair follicles, which then manifests clinically as delayed diffuse alopecia.

evidence.snippet: A trichogram revealed 95% of the hairs in dystrophic anagen status

nodes.description: Thallium has affinity for keratin-rich tissues and accumulates in hair and nails, establishing a distinct appendage-toxicity branch of poisoning.

evidence.snippet: Thallium does not have an internal anatomic reservoir, but has an affinity to keratin.

nodes.description: Thallium poisoning prominently injures the peripheral nervous system, producing a rapidly progressive, extremely painful ascending neuropathy and leaving persistent neurologic sequelae in some survivors.

evidence.snippet: The major manifestations of toxicity consist of a rapidly progressive, ascending, extremely painful sensory neuropathy and alopecia.
Neurologic damage represented the main sequelae of thallium poisoning in our present case report.

nodes.description: Thallium poisoning commonly causes a rapidly progressive, painful, ascending sensory-predominant peripheral neuropathy that may leave persistent neurologic deficits.

evidence.snippet: The major manifestations of toxicity consist of a rapidly progressive, ascending, extremely painful sensory neuropathy and alopecia.

nodes.description: Thallium enters cells through potassium-handling pathways because its ionic charge and radius closely resemble potassium, disrupting intracellular ionic homeostasis.

evidence.snippet: Thallium enters cells by a unique process governed by its similarity in charge and ionic radius to potassium.

nodes.description: Thallium inhibits sodium-potassium ATPase, destabilizing membrane ion homeostasis and contributing to autonomic and central neurotoxicity.

evidence.snippet: Additional effects include inhibition of sodium-potassium-adenosine triphosphatase

nodes.description: Thallium binds sulfhydryl-containing molecules, perturbing protein function and contributing to downstream neurotoxicity.
After absorption, thallium rapidly distributes throughout the body, including nervous tissue, skin, and hair, enabling multisystem toxicity.

evidence.snippet: Thallium compounds can accumulate in the human body and be rapidly distributed throughout it, including in the skin and hair.
